name: Restore Online Shoppers' Confidence Act (ROSCA)
summary: Federal law prohibiting deceptive online subscription enrollments and 'negative option' billing without clear consent.

evidence.claim: ROSCA (15 U.S.C. §§ 8401-8405) prohibits any post-transaction third-party seller from charging consumers without obtaining express informed consent and a billing-information transfer, and requires negative-option marketers to clearly disclose terms before obtaining payment information.
FTC enforcement under ROSCA includes Vonage 2022 settlement ($100M), Match.com 2019 lawsuit, MoviePass 2022 settlement; settlements typically require clear disclosure + simple cancellation.

body.scopeSummary: Three core protections: (1) prohibits post-transaction third-party data sharing for billing without express consent; (2) requires clear disclosure of negative-option terms before obtaining billing info; (3) requires simple cancellation mechanism. Unlike the vacated Click-to-Cancel Rule, ROSCA is statutory law not at risk of judicial reversal (E1).
body.userRightsPlainLanguage: If you signed up for an online free trial that auto-converted to paid without clear consent at the time of conversion, that's a ROSCA violation and the FTC can prosecute. ROSCA is the federal statutory backbone for online subscription consumer protection — it survived even after the Click-to-Cancel Rule was vacated.
